---
title: "Mapping the Charge: UK EV Infrastructure 2026"
date: 2026-01-10
categories: [Data Analysis, R, EV]
---
<!-- REST Endpoint for the specific dataset -->

## Introduction

As the UK transitions toward electric vehicles, understanding the density of charging infrastructure is critical. In this post, I explore the latest Department for Transport (DfT) data to see how many public chargers are available per 100,000 people across various Local Authorities.

## Data Acquisition

To ensure this analysis is reproducible, I am pulling the data directly from the ArcGIS REST API provided by the DfT GeoPortal. We use the `sf` library to read the GeoJSON stream. By setting `returnGeometry=false`, we fetch the data table quickly without the heavy geographic boundary files.

```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# API Endpoint for the Jan 2026 EV Charger Dataset
table_url <- "https://services5.arcgis.com/qUwnXV5uf1zyW6Y2/arcgis/rest/services/EVChargersEVCI0102_UK_DfT_Poly_2026_Jan/FeatureServer/0/query?outFields=*&where=1%3D1&f=geojson&returnGeometry=false"

# Read the data directly into a dataframe
ev_table <- st_read(table_url)

# Filter out the extreme outliers (> 1000)
ev_filtered <- ev_table %>% 
  filter(Public_EV_chargers_per_100k_pop <= 1000)

# Create the histogram
ggplot(ev_filtered, aes(x = Public_EV_chargers_per_100k_pop)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 35, fill = "#2c7fb8", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Public EV Chargers in the UK",
    subtitle = "Excludes Westminster and Hammersmith & Fulham (Outliers > 1000)",
    x = "EV Chargers per 100k People",
    y = "Number of Local Authorities"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```
